title: Add Minute Intervals
description: Add two minute quantities to find a total elapsed time.
condition: Two minute intervals are given (e.g., 25 minutes and 19 minutes) that together represent total elapsed time.
response: Add the minute values to find the total number of minutes.
label: Principle / Model

title: Add Minutes Across the Hour
description: Add a number of minutes to a given time, adjusting the hour when the total minutes reach or exceed 60.
condition: Given a start time in hours and minutes and an added number of minutes that causes the minutes to total 60 or more.
response: Add minutes, subtract 60 if needed, and increase the hour by 1.
label: Principle / Model
example: Starting at $5{:}43$, add 18 minutes: $43 + 18 = 61$ minutes, so $61 - 60 = 1$ minute and increase hour to 6 → $6{:}01$.

title: Compare Quantities by Subtraction
description: Find how much more one quantity is than another by subtracting the smaller from the larger.
condition: Given two quantities and asked how much more or heavier one is than the other.
response: Compute larger − smaller.
label: Principle / Model
example: To find how much heavier 835 kg is than 143 kg, compute $835 - 143 = 692$.

title: Compute Quotient in Equal Groups Context
description: Given a total and number of equal groups, divide to find the amount in each group.
condition: A total quantity and a number of equal groups are given; the amount in each group is unknown (e.g., 96 kg in 6 boxes).
response: Divide total by number of groups to find the amount per group (e.g., 96 ÷ 6 = 16).
label: Principle / Model
example: If 96 kg are placed equally into 6 boxes, compute $96 \div 6 = 16$. Each box has 16 kg.
notes: Division interpreted as finding an unknown factor.

title: Convert Mixed Number to Improper Fraction
description: Rewrite a mixed number as an equivalent improper fraction.
condition: A mixed number $a\tfrac{b}{c}$ is given.
response: Compute $\frac{ac+b}{c}$.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Used to express equivalent measurement forms.

title: Count Up to the Next Hour
description: Find the number of minutes needed to reach the next full hour from a given time.
condition: A start time includes minutes past the hour and the strategy is to count up to the next full hour (e.g., 9:35 a.m.).
response: Subtract the current minutes from 60 to determine minutes to the next hour.
label: Principle / Model

title: Determine Elapsed Time Across an Hour
description: Compute elapsed time when the end time is in the next hour.
condition: Given a start time and end time that cross an hour boundary (e.g., 9:35 a.m. to 10:19 a.m.).
response: Count up to the next hour, then add remaining minutes to reach the end time.
label: Principle / Model
example: 9:35 → 10:00 is 25 minutes; 10:00 → 10:19 is 19 minutes; total $25 + 19 = 44$ minutes.

title: Estimate Objects About 1 Gram
description: Recognize or estimate which everyday objects have a mass of about 1 gram using known benchmarks (e.g., small light items like a piece of gum or pen cap).
condition: Given a list of everyday objects and asked which weigh about 1 gram.
response: Select objects whose typical mass is about 1 gram.
label: Production / Skill
example: From the list {piece of gum, notebook, door key, pen cap, toothbrush}, select piece of gum and pen cap as about $1$ gram.
notes: Relies on benchmark sense of 1 gram rather than exact calculation.

title: Estimate Objects About 1 Kilogram
description: Recognize or estimate which everyday objects have a mass of about 1 kilogram using familiar reference objects.
condition: Given a list of objects and asked which weighs about 1 kilogram.
response: Select the object whose typical mass is about 1 kilogram.
label: Production / Skill
example: From the list {1 blueberry, 1 pineapple, 1 grape, 1 raspberry}, select 1 pineapple as about $1$ kilogram.
notes: Depends on experiential knowledge of kilogram benchmarks.

title: Identify Equal Groups in a Diagram
description: Recognize a visual representation as showing a specific number of equal groups with a given size.
condition: A diagram shows repeated identical quantities (e.g., seven boxes each labeled 2).
response: Interpret the diagram as a specific number of groups of equal size (e.g., 7 groups of 2).
label: Production / Skill
example: A diagram showing seven boxes each labeled 2 represents $7$ groups of $2$.

title: Identify Fractional Tick Marks on a Number Line
description: Determine fractional values (such as halves and fourths) represented by evenly spaced tick marks between whole numbers.
condition: Given a number line with evenly spaced tick marks between labeled whole numbers.
response: Assign fractional values (e.g., $\frac{1}{2}$, $\frac{1}{4}$, $\frac{3}{4}$) to intermediate ticks.
label: Principle / Model
example: Between 4 and 5 divided into fourths, the third tick after 4 represents $4\frac{3}{4}$.

title: Identify Half Liter Between Whole Numbers
description: Recognize that one-half liter is located halfway between two consecutive whole-number liter markings when the scale is partitioned into halves.
condition: Given a measurement scale marked in whole liters and evenly partitioned into halves, and asked to locate $n \tfrac{1}{2}$ liters.
response: Identify the point halfway between $n$ and $n+1$ liters.
label: Principle / Model
example: $3\tfrac{1}{2}$ liters is halfway between $3$ and $4$ liters on a scale divided into halves.
notes: Requires understanding of fractions on a number line.

title: Identify Quarter-Inch Tick Marks
description: Recognize that each inch on a ruler is divided into four equal parts representing fourths (1/4 inch increments).
condition: A ruler marked with equally spaced smaller tick marks between whole numbers.
response: State that each inch is divided into fourths and that each small tick represents 1/4 inch.
label: Concept
example: Between 2 and 3 inches, there are four equal parts: $2, 2\tfrac{1}{4}, 2\tfrac{1}{2}, 2\tfrac{3}{4}, 3$.
notes: Supports measuring to the nearest 1/4 inch.

title: Interpret Tick Mark Values on a Scale
description: Determine the value represented by each tick mark on a measurement scale by analyzing how the interval between labeled numbers is partitioned.
condition: Given a vertical scale with labeled whole numbers and evenly spaced tick marks between them.
response: Determine the fractional value each tick mark represents (e.g., halves, fourths).
label: Principle / Model
example: If there are two equal intervals between $3$ and $4$, each tick represents $\tfrac{1}{2}$ liter; if four equal intervals, each represents $\tfrac{1}{4}$ liter.
notes: Essential for distinguishing correct representations of $3\tfrac{1}{2}$ liters from misleading halfway positions.

title: Locate Minute Mark on Analog Clock
description: Place the minute hand at the correct tick mark corresponding to a given number of minutes.
condition: A specific minute value between 0 and 59 is given.
response: Position the minute hand on the corresponding tick mark (each small tick = 1 minute).
label: Production / Skill
example: For 49 minutes, place the minute hand one tick before the 10 (which represents 50 minutes).

title: Match Equal-Groups Diagram to Unknown Product Situation
description: Select a word problem that matches a diagram showing known number of groups and known group size with unknown total.
condition: A diagram shows known groups and known size per group, with the total unknown.
response: Choose the situation describing multiplication with unknown product (e.g., 7 bottles with 2 liters each, total unknown).
label: Rule / Plan
example: A diagram of $7$ groups of $2$ matches: "There are 7 bottles. Each has 2 liters. How many liters are there altogether?"
notes: Distinguishes unknown product from unknown factor situations.

title: Match Liquid Level to Target Volume
description: Compare the height of liquid in a container to the interpreted scale markings to determine whether it equals a specified volume.
condition: Given a container image with a marked scale and a target volume (e.g., $3\tfrac{1}{2}$ liters).
response: Select containers where the liquid level corresponds to the target volume based on the scale.
label: Production / Skill
example: Select containers where the liquid reaches the tick corresponding to $3\tfrac{1}{2}$ liters.
notes: Integrates fraction understanding with scale interpretation.

title: Measure Length to Nearest Quarter Inch
description: Determine the length of an object by aligning it with a ruler and reading the nearest quarter-inch mark.
condition: An object aligned with 0 on a ruler marked in fourths, with the endpoint between whole numbers.
response: State the object’s length as a whole number or mixed number to the nearest 1/4 inch.
label: Production / Skill
example: If the pencil ends at the first tick after 5, report $5\tfrac{1}{4}$ inches (or $\tfrac{21}{4}$ inches).
notes: Requires visual alignment and interpretation of tick marks.

title: Place Hour Hand Between Numbers
description: Position the hour hand between two numbers according to the number of minutes past the hour.
condition: A time includes minutes past the hour (not exactly :00).
response: Place the hour hand between the current hour and the next hour, proportionally closer to the next hour as minutes increase.
label: Production / Skill
example: At 11:49, place the hour hand between 11 and 12, very close to 12.

title: Plot Data on Line Plot
description: Represent each data value with a mark above its corresponding position on a number line.
condition: Given a set of numerical measurements and a number line with matching scale.
response: Place one X (or mark) above the corresponding value for each measurement.
label: Production / Skill
example: For lengths $2\frac{1}{2}, 2\frac{1}{2}, 2\frac{3}{4}$, place two X's above $2\frac{1}{2}$ and one above $2\frac{3}{4}$.

title: Read Ruler Measurement to Fraction
description: Determine the length of an object using a ruler marked in fractional units.
condition: Given an object aligned to 0 on a ruler marked in quarter inches.
response: State the length as a mixed number using the nearest tick mark.
label: Production / Skill
example: If the object ends at the third quarter mark past 4, the length is $4\frac{3}{4}$ inches.

title: Read Time on Analog Clock
description: Determine the hour and minute shown by the hour and minute hands on an analog clock.
condition: Given an analog clock with hour and minute hands.
response: State the time in hours and minutes.
label: Production / Skill
example: If the minute hand points to 43 minutes and the hour hand is between 5 and 6, the time is $5{:}43$.

title: Recognize 60 Minutes in One Hour
description: Know that one hour is composed of 60 minutes.
condition: Reasoning about relationships between hours and minutes.
response: State or use the fact that 1 hour = 60 minutes.
label: Fact
example: $1$ hour $= 60$ minutes.

title: Set Analog Clock to Given Time
description: Coordinate placement of both hour and minute hands to represent a specific digital time.
condition: A specific time is given in hours and minutes (e.g., 11:49 a.m.).
response: Place the minute hand at the correct minute mark and the hour hand at the correct position relative to the hour.
label: Production / Skill
example: For 11:49 a.m., minute hand at 49 minutes; hour hand just before 12.
notes: Composite of minute and hour placement skills.

title: Subtract Within 1,000 Using Place Value
description: Subtract multi-digit numbers within 1,000 using place value reasoning or decomposition.
condition: Given two numbers less than 1,000 to subtract.
response: Subtract by decomposing hundreds, tens, and ones or using standard algorithm.
label: Principle / Model

title: Use Distributive Strategy to Multiply
description: Break apart one factor to compute a product using easier partial products.
condition: A multiplication problem where one factor can be decomposed (e.g., 6 × 16).
response: Decompose and compute partial products, then add (e.g., 6 × 10 + 6 × 6 = 60 + 36 = 96).
label: Principle / Model
example: To compute $6 \times 16$, use $6 \times (10 + 6) = 6 \times 10 + 6 \times 6 = 60 + 36 = 96$.

title: Verify Division Using Multiplication
description: Check a division result by multiplying the quotient by the divisor to see if it equals the dividend.
condition: A quotient has been found for a division problem.
response: Multiply quotient by divisor to confirm it equals the original total.
label: Principle / Model
example: After finding $96 \div 6 = 16$, check by computing $6 \times 16 = 96$.
notes: Used to justify correctness of unknown factor solution.
